# **Computational Framework for Transfer Learning in Single-Cell Perturbation Biology: Technical Report on LINCS L1000 and chemCPA Training for V8 Stage 2 Execution**

## **Paradigm Shift in Virtual Screening and Single-Cell Perturbation Modeling**

Mapping the causal linkages between chemical perturbations and downstream transcriptomic profiles at single-cell resolution represents a critical objective in translational biology and pharmaceutical discovery.1 High-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq HTS) screens are highly informative, but they are constrained by significant library preparation costs and a limited scale that typically restricts testing to fewer than 200 distinct compounds.3 To bridge this gap, computational architectures must generalize to unseen chemical structures.3  
The Connectivity Map (CMap) developed by the Broad Institute represents a substantial step forward, comprising over 1.3 million small-molecule perturbation measurements.2 However, the utility of the standard CMap database is bottlenecked by its measurement of only 978 landmark genes, which constitute less than five percent of the human genome, and its near-exclusive reliance on cancer cell lines that may not faithfully represent physiological human biology.2 Consequently, pretraining deep generative architectures such as the Chemical Compositional Perturbation Autoencoder (chemCPA) on these large bulk repositories, and subsequently transferring this learned representation to resolve single-cell expression profiles, unblocks the real-data execution stage (Stage 2\) of the V8 discovery pipeline.4

## **Mathematical and Architectural Foundations of chemCPA**

The chemCPA architecture is structured around three core modular assemblies: a gene expression encoder-decoder network, an attribute embedding system, and adversarial classifiers.3 Let ![][image1] represent the measured ![][image2]\-dimensional gene expression profile of cell ![][image3].3 The encoding network ![][image4], parameterized by ![][image5], maps this expression profile to an ![][image6]\-dimensional latent basal state vector ![][image7].3 Through adversarial training, ![][image8] is trained to discard all information pertaining to covariate attributes ![][image9].3 To reconstruct the perturbed transcriptomic state, the latent representation is modified through an additive composition, yielding a perturbed state ![][image10] defined by the formula:  
![][image11]  
where ![][image12] is the drug perturbation vector and ![][image13] represents a learnable embedding of covariates such as cell lines.3 The drug perturbation network ![][image14] leverages a pre-trained molecule encoder ![][image15], a dosage scaler ![][image16], and a perturbation encoder ![][image17].3 First, ![][image15] maps chemical structures (SMILES) to a fixed chemical descriptor ![][image18].3 Second, the dosage scaler ![][image16] computes the effective scaled dosage ![][image19] from the input dosage ![][image20].3 Finally, the perturbation encoder ![][image17] projects this to the latent perturbation vector ![][image21].3  
The decoder ![][image22], parameterized by ![][image23], translates the perturbed state ![][image10] into the component-wise mean ![][image24] and variance ![][image25] of normal distributions.3 While the model supports both negative binomial and Gaussian likelihood frameworks, the Gaussian likelihood shows superior empirical convergence on log-transformed counts, leading to its selection as the default reconstruction loss.5 The reconstruction loss ![][image26] is computed as:  
![][image27]  
To achieve representation disentanglement, chemCPA deploys auxiliary multi-layer perceptron classifiers ![][image28] and ![][image29], parameterized by ![][image30], which are optimized to predict the drug identity ![][image31] and the cell line covariate ![][image32] from ![][image8].5 The cross-entropy classification losses are minimized by the classifiers, while the encoder ![][image33] is trained via min-max optimization to maximize classifier error.3 A zero-centered gradient penalty ![][image34] is applied to stabilize the discriminative gradient flow:  
![][image35]  
The training process alternates between updating the autoencoder to minimize ![][image36] and updating the adversarial classifiers to minimize ![][image37].5 During pretraining, several molecular encoders ![][image15] can be integrated.3 To ensure reproducibility across execution runs, the pretrained weights associated with specific molecular encoders are tracked via unique MD5 hashes, as outlined in Table 1\.11

| Molecular Encoder | MD5 Hash | Underlying Architecture / Embeddings | Role in chemCPA Pipeline |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| grover\_base | ff420aea264fca7668ecb147f60762a1 | Graph-based Transformer.11 | Extracts high-dimensional graph representations of molecules.11 |
| jtvae | a7060ac4e2c6154e64a13acd414cbba2 | Junction Tree Variational Autoencoder.11 | Captures structural scaffolds and chemical substructures.11 |
| rdkit | 4f061dbfc7af05cf84f06a724b0c8563 | Hand-crafted descriptors.11 | Generates classic physicochemical and fingerprint descriptors.11 |

## **LINCS L1000 Dataset Profiling and Data Engineering**

The LINCS L1000 project processes raw transcriptomic measurements through five distinct data levels to convert fluorescence intensities into robust differential signatures.14 The primary characteristics, standard file suffixes, and their precise role within the pretraining and fine-tuning pipelines are detailed in Table 2\.14

| Data Level | Standard File Suffix | Biological and Technical Description | Pipeline Integration and Utility |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Level 1 (LXB)** | .lxb | Raw flow cytometry bead fluorescence intensities from Luminex scanners.14 | Excluded from pretraining due to lack of landmark gene mapping and massive file size.14 |
| **Level 2 (GEX)** | Per-well expression | Absolute gene expression levels of 1,000 genes after deconvolution from Luminex beads.14 | Serves as an unnormalized intermediate state in processing.14 |
| **Level 3 (Q2NORM)** | .gctx / .h5ad | Quantile-normalized expression profiles of landmark transcripts and inferred genes.14 | Ingested to generate baseline models and absolute expression reconstruction matrices.14 |
| **Level 4 (Z-SCORES)** | .gctx | Signatures with robust z-scores relative to plate population or vehicle control.14 | Optional input for delta-expression modeling and directionality mapping.14 |
| **Level 5 (DGE)** | Collapsed signatures | Differential gene expression signatures computed using the characteristic direction method.14 | Utilized as a reference baseline to evaluate fold changes and pathway responses.14 |

Data engineering operations on the LINCS Level 3 .gctx file require strict parsing to extract and isolate row names, column names, and normalized expression matrices.16 During the generation of the signature and instance metadata files, technical batch effects must be corrected.1 The batch extraction logic is standardized based on file naming patterns.16 For example, given a signature ID such as AML001\_CD34\_24H:BRD-A03772856:0.37037, the system extracts the text preceding the colon to isolate the batch ID AML001\_CD34\_24H.16 Similarly, for individual sample IDs like ERG013\_VCAP\_72H\_X3\_B11, the first three underscore-delimited terms are extracted, yielding the batch ID ERG013\_VCAP\_72H.16  
Inconsistencies between different iterations of the preprocessing pipeline—such as between lincs\_pp.ipynb and the more comprehensive lincs\_full\_pp.ipynb—are resolved by aligning these batch mappings, generating standard lincs\_small\_.h5ad and lincs\_complete.h5ad AnnData objects containing annotated covariates.17

## **Runtime Environments and Execution Frameworks**

To maintain reproducibility across varying hardware configurations and unblock V8 Stage 2 execution, the chemCPA code is deployed within a standardized Dockerized container.12 The container can be instantiated using the execution command:  
docker run \-it \-p 8888:8888 \--platform=linux/amd64 registry.hf.space/b1ro-chemcpa:latest.12  
This image pre-installs the underlying Anaconda environments, CUDA libraries, and the chemCPA package dependencies.12 However, raw datasets are excluded from the container and are downloaded dynamically using the utility script raw\_data/dataset.py or through manual link resolution.12  
When running a distributed parameter sweep on cluster systems, the entry function is mapped to ExperimentWrapper.\_\_init\_\_ located within the execution wrapper script chemCPA/seml\_sweep\_icb.py.12 For local validation and debugging, developers can bypass MongoDB and SLURM overheads by utilizing the manual execution script chemCPA/manual\_seml\_sweep.py (which internally references manual\_run.yaml).11 This script trains the autoencoder on local hardware, verifying that the data loaders and gradient update loops operate correctly before full cluster deployment.11

## **Pretraining Hyperparameter Profiles**

The tuning of hyperparameters during pretraining is governed by the configuration sweeps defined in experiments/lincs\_rdkit\_hparam.11 These sweeps ensure that the autoencoder reaches an optimal trade-off between reconstruction accuracy and latent representation disentanglement.5 The default parameter structure for executing the LINCS L1000 pretraining runs is detailed in Table 3\.21

| Hyperparameter Parameter | YAML Configuration Attribute | Operational Value | Structural Influence on Model Training |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Latent Dimension (![][image6])** | model.hparams.dim | 32 21 | Specifies the dimension of the shared representation space.3 |
| **Dropout Rate** | model.hparams.dropout | 0.262378 21 | Prevents co-adaptation and overfitting on sparse transcriptomes.21 |
| **Autoencoder Width** | model.hparams.autoencoder\_width | 256 21 | Regulates layer-wise capacity of the encoder and decoder.3 |
| **Autoencoder Depth** | model.hparams.autoencoder\_depth | 4 21 | Sets the number of layers in the fully connected MLPs.3 |
| **Learning Rate (AE)** | autoencoder\_lr | 0.001121 21 | Step size for the Adam optimizer updating the AE and perturbation networks.21 |
| **Batch Size** | model.hparams.batch\_size | Choice options (e.g., 256\) 21 | Dictates the size of mini-batches sampled during stochastic training.21 |
| **Disentanglement Weight** | model.hparams.dim\_adversary | Balanced choice 5 | Scales the multi-class adversarial classification loss.5 |

## **Transfer Learning, Gene Set Alignment, and Comparative Model Benchmarks**

Transitioning the pre-trained chemCPA model from bulk L1000 data to single-cell assays (such as the sci-Plex3 dataset) requires careful gene set alignment and covariate mapping.5 Because LINCS L1000 is restricted to 978 landmark genes, fine-tuning on an expanded single-cell transcriptomic set (e.g., 2,000 highly variable genes) requires modifying the input of the encoder ![][image33] and the output layer of the decoder ![][image38].6 While the newly introduced genes are trained from scratch, the frozen molecular embedding weights in ![][image14] transfer chemical knowledge across domains.4  
Additionally, covariates missing from the bulk pretraining data, such as the K562 cancer cell line, are accommodated by retraining the cell embedding dictionary from scratch.25 This enables the model to map historical chemical patterns to novel biological contexts.5 To understand how chemCPA performs relative to other models, Table 4 benchmarks its predictive accuracy against alternative state-of-the-art frameworks.13

| Model Framework | Primary Architecture | Transfer Capabilities | Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Performance |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **chemCPA (Pretrained)** | Compositional Autoencoder with MLP encoders.3 | Integrates bulk L1000 pretraining to generalize to unseen drugs.4 | Strong generalization on landmark genes (![][image39] at 10 ![][image40]).13 |
| **Biolord** | Latent optimization framework.13 | Captures pathway structures without explicit bulk pretraining.13 | Outperforms chemCPA on sci-Plex3 OOD test sets (![][image39] at 10 ![][image40]).13 |
| **PerturbNet** | Flexible deep generative model.26 | Incorporates gene annotations and sequence embeddings.26 | Captures distributions of genetic and chemical perturbations.26 |
| **scAgents** | Large Language Model-based agent.27 | Adapts across multiple modalities (ATAC, CITE-seq).27 | Achieves a 20% increase in Pearson correlation over chemCPA.27 |
| **scDCA** | Drug-conditional adapter on foundation models.28 | Fine-tunes ![][image41] of pre-trained weights (scGPT).28 | Strong zero-shot generalization across drug target clusters.28 |

## **Inference Diagnostics, Prediction Functions, and Evaluation Diagnostics**

Executing prediction and validation within the chemCPA framework relies on a precise computational pathway.30 Within train.py, a boolean flag bool\_de identifies differentially expressed genes based on the array de\_genes, which is keyed on the specific cell\_drug\_dose\_comb attribute.31 The evaluation function compute\_prediction (found at line 361 in experiments\_run.py and line 194 in train.py) executes as follows:  
![][image42]  
30  
A critical detail is that the tensor genes\_control contains unperturbed control expressions pooled across multiple cell lines (such as A549, MCF7, and K562).30 This pooled tensor is passed during evaluation to allow the model to loop over all drug, cell line, and dosage categories (dataset.pert\_categories), extracting the matching control background and applying the corresponding drug embedding emb\_drugs and cell line covariate embedding emb\_covs.30 The resulting mean prediction ![][image43] is compared to the mean of the actual treated test sub-dataset.30 Evaluating predictions strictly on their mean values can be limiting, and using the Wasserstein distance has been proposed to evaluate the full predicted single-cell distribution variance.30  
For inference on novel, unseen compounds, the predict function accepts drug embeddings, covariate configurations, control gene expression data, and a model checkpoint.32 This process is illustrated in the prepare function for the lincs\_full\_sciplex\_genes dataset.32 However, a key engineering challenge is that the setup logic of the model automatically attempts to load the specific molecular embeddings used during pretraining, which can cause runtime crashes if those exact files are missing from the disk.32  
Predictive accuracy is typically evaluated using the coefficient of determination (![][image44]) on both the full gene set and the subset of highly variable differentially expressed genes (DEGs).10 Because most genes are unaffected by any given perturbation, models that discard drug information (using only unperturbed controls) can still achieve high ![][image44] scores across the whole gene set.10 Thus, evaluating performance on DEGs is critical to determine whether the model is capturing true chemical effects.8  
In single-drug experiments, nine compounds are held out as OOD: Dacinostat, Givinostat, Belinostat, Hesperadin, Quisinostat, Alvespimycin, Tanespimycin, TAK-901, and Flavopiridol.8 Training the model with low-dose observations (e.g., 1 uM or 10 uM) of these OOD compounds significantly reduces their predictive accuracy on high-dose effects compared to holding them out entirely, indicating that clear, high-dose perturbation signals are crucial for the model to learn stable latent drug representations.8 In multi-drug combination tasks, holding out combinations involving Panobinostat yields high accuracy (![][image45] on DEGs) for combinations with drugs such as SRT2104, but fails on the combination of Alvespimycin and Panobinostat (![][image46]) due to a severe, unmodeled transcriptional distribution shift.8
